subject: Make stand-alone Policy environment
commit_message:
Make stand-alone Policy environment

This is a first cut at making it possible to easily run Policy
components within a stand-alone environment.  Made the following
changes:
- enhanced wait_for_port.sh to accept a list of hosts/ports
- enhanced wait_for_port.sh to accept an optional command to execute
  when the wait completes
- consolidated all of the yml files into a single file
- removed "extra" services from the yml so that a component and its
  dependents can be brought up in a single invocation
- modified the component setup.sh

With these changes, a developer can clone the csit repo, run detmVers.sh
to set the docker image version env variables, and then bring up a
component (and its dependents) using:

  docker-compose -f scripts/policy/docker-compose-all.yml
    up --detach <component>

Note: this does not preload or deploy any policies; that must be done
manually via curl.

Modified to use new simulator docker image.
Also fixed drools-apps CSIT broken due to disabling frankfurt
controller.
Also removed operational.Apex policy. The csar file used in
policy-distribution test is also updated to reflect this change.

Issue-ID: POLICY-2742
Change-Id: I412dbd5db9219ad2cdece9693f0b4b9fe7b2eb69
Signed-off-by: Jim Hahn <jrh3@att.com>
Signed-off-by: a.sreekumar <ajith.sreekumar@bell.ca>

my note is to bring up drools-apps CSIT env is: 
running the following three docker-compose:
++ docker-compose -f /root/csit/scripts/policy/drools-apps/docker-compose-drools-apps.yml run --rm start_dependencies
++ docker-compose -f /root/csit/scripts/policy/drools-apps/docker-compose-drools-apps.yml run --rm start_pap
++ docker-compose -f /root/csit/scripts/policy/drools-apps/docker-compose-drools-apps.yml run --rm start_all

- bring down docker-compose
docker-compose -f /root/csit/scripts/policy/drools-apps/docker-compose-drools-apps.yml down -v

also have to pull the docker images, and tag correctly associate with "image" in docker-compose file.

Patch Set 7: Code-Review+1

I'm marking this for review.  However, if folks don't feel like this is a good approach, then we can reject it and take a different approach when I return.  For minor issues, feel free to patch it and merge it - no need to wait for me to get back.

With this, the S3P tests can be modified to use the CSIT, using drools-compose to bring up the relevant component(s).  As for moving the CSITs into the individual repos, I'm still a bit unsure about that, as there is a lot of common code between the CSITs for each component.  We'll have to come up with a good way to share code (perhaps leave the common code in integration/csit?) between the repos.  I also don't want CSIT failures to prevent jenkins verification or merge jobs from succeeding, otherwise we might wind up in a catch-22 situation.

Patch Set 7:

Nice work!
I am only a bit concerned about the simulators, in most cases we only need message-router. Here since all are in one container, there is the overhead to run everything, also we can't name the service as message-router, and hence making it necessary to mount the defaultConfig files for all components.
But maybe its a tradeoff. This definitely make the scripts clean and easy to use.

Patch Set 7:

(1 comment)

For a standalone policy environment, I think it would be better if possible to have it in the policy/docker repo as what we had in earlier releases although unmaintained.   Having it in the integration/csit, besides not having full control (we don't own the repo), poses the additional overhead of depending on the csit software framework, which again we don't have control over it, nor it would be necessary for a standalone docker-compose based environment.   A thought to consider.

Patch Set 7:

> Patch Set 7:
> 
> Nice work!
> I am only a bit concerned about the simulators, in most cases we only need message-router. Here since all are in one container, there is the overhead to run everything, also we can't name the service as message-router, and hence making it necessary to mount the defaultConfig files for all components.
> But maybe its a tradeoff. This definitely make the scripts clean and easy to use.

Don't think there's much overhead in running the extra simulators since it's all in one JVM; the convenience of not having to configure which simulator to run seemed worth the trade-off.

Patch Set 7:

(2 comments)

> Patch Set 7:
> 
> (1 comment)
> 
> For a standalone policy environment, I think it would be better if possible to have it in the policy/docker repo as what we had in earlier releases although unmaintained.   Having it in the integration/csit, besides not having full control (we don't own the repo), poses the additional overhead of depending on the csit software framework, which again we don't have control over it, nor it would be necessary for a standalone docker-compose based environment.   A thought to consider.

Figured we'd move things around in the next round of reviews for this.  Is policy/docker an appropriate place for this (seems kind of like creating a circular dependency, but probably ok in this case)?  As for our CSITs, it makes sense to me that we'd distribute them among the various repos, but I don't want a CSIT failure to prevent a verify or merge job from completing successfully or we could wind up in a "catch-22" situation.  Not sure if we can do that easily, or if we should just set up a policy/csit repo.

Patch Set 7:

Should we move forward with this?

I'm ok with this - I think we can also propagate some of the work to policy/docker. The Dockerfile's and scripts could just live there for use outside of integration/csit. As well as, I'm sure at some point the Integration team will formally remove CSIT from ONAP CI chain.

Still to be done: 1) push the simulator docker image as part of the models jenkins job  2) move stuff from csit to policy/docker, replacing robot scripts with python scripts  3) add helm charts to policy/docker (assuming it's needed) so a k8s environment, including the simulator, can be easily brought up for S3P testing
